title: Cambridge Challenge
seoDescription: Take a fast Cambridge-style reasoning quiz with staged visual logic, number patterns, deduction, and analytical puzzles.
pageTitle: Only 4% Can Pass This Cambridge Challenge
summary: A staged 80-question challenge inspired by clear reasoning, careful judgement, and problem-solving.

homepage.title: Can You Pass This Cambridge Challenge?
homepage.summary: Think you can handle 10 rounds of clear academic reasoning?

footer.aboutText: This Cambridge-style challenge is a fast reasoning quiz built around clear thinking, logical judgement, evidence reading, number sense, and problem-solving. The University of Cambridge, founded in 1209, is known worldwide for academic depth, historic buildings, and long traditions of careful discussion. This quiz uses that atmosphere as inspiration for staged questions that feel thoughtful but still fun. It is designed for entertainment, curiosity, and self-challenge, not as an official University of Cambridge examination or official assessment.

profiles.title: You thought like a strong problem solver.
profiles.copy: Exceptional reasoning across the full challenge. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Strong performance.
profiles.copy: You stayed sharp as the questions became more demanding. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Good score.
profiles.copy: You handled the core reasoning rounds well. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Keep sharpening.
profiles.copy: The later rounds reward calm pattern recognition and careful logic. Your best area was {stage}.

stageGroups.title: Quick Warm Up
stageGroups.encouragement: Good start. The first round was about fast, clear thinking. Next, the patterns get a little sharper.

questions.prompt: Which item does not belong with the others?
questions.explanation: A pencil is used for writing, while the others are used for eating or serving food.

questions.prompt: If 2 + 3 = 5, what is 5 + 4?
questions.explanation: 5 plus 4 equals 9.

questions.prompt: Which word best completes the idea: book is to reading as fork is to ____?
questions.explanation: A book is commonly used for reading, and a fork is commonly used for eating.

questions.prompt: A student has three pencils and gives one away. How many pencils remain?
questions.explanation: Starting with 3 and giving away 1 leaves 2.

questions.prompt: Which shape has three sides?
questions.explanation: A triangle is defined by having three sides.

questions.prompt: Which statement is most clearly true?
questions.explanation: A week has seven days; the other statements are not generally true.

questions.prompt: Which number comes next: 2, 4, 6, 8, ____?
questions.explanation: The sequence increases by 2 each time, so the next number is 10.

questions.prompt: A door is locked. Which object is most likely to open it?
questions.explanation: A key is the object normally used to open a locked door.

stageGroups.title: Spot the Simple Pattern
stageGroups.encouragement: Nice work. You are warming up quickly. Now the questions start asking for a little more attention.

questions.prompt: Which number is the odd one out?
questions.explanation: 19 is odd, while the other numbers are even.

questions.prompt: Which pair follows the same relationship as hot and cold?
questions.explanation: Hot and cold are opposites, and light and dark are also opposites.

questions.prompt: What comes next: red, blue, red, blue, ____?
questions.explanation: The colours alternate red and blue, so the next one is red.

questions.prompt: If every square is a shape, which statement must be true?
questions.explanation: The statement directly says that every square is a shape.

questions.prompt: Which is the largest number?
questions.explanation: 0.8 is greater than 0.75, 0.6, and 0.55.

questions.prompt: A bag contains only apples. You take one item from the bag. What must it be?
questions.explanation: If the bag contains only apples, any item taken from it must be an apple.

questions.prompt: Which set is in the correct order from smallest to largest?
questions.explanation: 1, 2, 3 is the ascending order.

questions.prompt: If a train leaves at 10:00 and the journey takes 30 minutes, when does it arrive?
questions.explanation: Thirty minutes after 10:00 is 10:30.

stageGroups.title: Everyday Logic
stageGroups.encouragement: That round tested common sense and clean reasoning. The next stage adds slightly more careful choices.

questions.prompt: Maya studies for 20 minutes, rests for 10 minutes, then studies for 20 more minutes. How much study time is that?
questions.explanation: Only the two study periods count, so 20 plus 20 is 40 minutes.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion follows best: All library books must be returned. This book is from the library.
questions.explanation: If all library books must be returned and this is a library book, it must be returned.

questions.prompt: Which choice is most like a timetable?
questions.explanation: A timetable is a plan or list of scheduled times.

questions.prompt: Which number completes the pattern: 5, 10, 15, 20, ____?
questions.explanation: The pattern increases by 5 each time, so the next number is 25.

questions.prompt: Container A is fuller than Container B. Container B is fuller than Container C. Which is fullest?
questions.explanation: Container A is fuller than Container B, and Container B is fuller than Container C.

questions.prompt: Which instruction is most precise?
questions.explanation: It gives a specific page and a specific task.

questions.prompt: If one ticket costs 4 units, how much do three tickets cost?
questions.explanation: Three tickets cost 3 times 4, which is 12.

questions.prompt: A researcher wants the most reliable result. What should they do?
questions.explanation: Reliable conclusions are stronger when based on enough evidence.

stageGroups.title: Smart Choices
stageGroups.encouragement: You are now thinking like a problem-solver. Next comes a round of patterns and careful comparisons.

questions.prompt: A recipe for 2 people uses 4 eggs. How many eggs are needed for 4 people?
questions.explanation: Doubling the number of people doubles the eggs from 4 to 8.

questions.prompt: Which sentence gives evidence rather than opinion?
choices: The test score increased by 10 points
questions.explanation: A measured increase in a score is evidence, not just personal opinion.

questions.prompt: Which item would be most useful for measuring length?
questions.explanation: A ruler is designed to measure length.

questions.prompt: A box is heavier than a bag. The bag is heavier than a book. Which is lightest?
questions.explanation: The book is lighter than the bag, and the bag is lighter than the box.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 1, 3, 6, 10, ____?
questions.explanation: The increases are 2, 3, and 4, so the next increase is 5, giving 15.

questions.prompt: Which question is best for testing cause and effect?
choices: What happens if the temperature rises?
questions.explanation: Asking what happens after a change tests cause and effect.

questions.prompt: Four students score 7, 8, 8, and 9. What is the average score?
questions.explanation: The total is 32, and 32 divided by 4 is 8.

questions.prompt: Which plan is most likely to reduce mistakes?
questions.explanation: Reviewing answers gives a chance to catch and correct mistakes.

stageGroups.title: Pattern Playground
stageGroups.encouragement: Halfway through, and you are still in the game. From here, the questions become more layered.

questions.prompt: Which number completes the sequence: 3, 6, 12, 24, ____?
questions.explanation: Each number is doubled, so 24 becomes 48.

questions.prompt: A code changes 2 to 6 and 4 to 12. What would it change 5 to?
questions.explanation: The rule is multiply by 3, so 5 becomes 15.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is strongest?
choices: There is no evidence against it
One person says it is true
questions.explanation: Evidence gives stronger support than opinion, possibility, or lack of disproof.

questions.prompt: If all glims are vors, and no vors are tazes, what must be true?
questions.explanation: If glims are inside the vor group and vors cannot be tazes, glims cannot be tazes.

questions.prompt: Which set follows the same pattern as 2, 5, 8?
questions.explanation: 2, 5, 8 increases by 3 each time, and 1, 4, 7 does the same.

questions.prompt: A notice says: Submit the form before step 4. Which action follows the notice?
questions.explanation: Step 2 comes before step 4, so it follows the instruction.

questions.prompt: Which result would most weaken the claim: Exercise improved memory in the group?
choices: The group slept less than usual
A similar group improved without exercise
questions.explanation: If a similar group improved without exercise, the improvement may not be caused by exercise.

questions.prompt: A square garden has four equal sides. If one side is 6 units, what is the perimeter?
questions.explanation: A square has four equal sides, so 4 times 6 is 24 units.

stageGroups.title: Flexible Thinking
stageGroups.encouragement: Now the challenge becomes more layered. Stay calm: every answer is still reachable with clear thinking.

questions.prompt: A student argues, 'This idea is old, so it must be wrong.' What is the main flaw?
choices: The idea has too many words
Age alone does not prove truth or falsehood
questions.explanation: Whether an idea is old does not by itself show whether it is right or wrong.

questions.prompt: Which number best completes the pattern: 4, 9, 19, 39, ____?
questions.explanation: Each term doubles and adds 1: 4 to 9, 9 to 19, 19 to 39, so 39 becomes 79.

questions.prompt: A class has 30 students. Two thirds choose biology. How many choose biology?
questions.explanation: Two thirds of 30 is 20.

questions.prompt: Which statement is an assumption in this argument: 'The session is popular because the room is full.'
choices: The room is full because people came to the session
questions.explanation: The argument assumes the full room is caused by interest in the session.

questions.prompt: A scientist changes one factor at a time during an experiment. Why?
choices: To make the result easier to compare
questions.explanation: Changing one factor at a time helps identify what caused any change in results.

questions.prompt: Which is the best summary of: 'More data can improve a conclusion, but only if the data is relevant and reliable'?
choices: Data quality matters as well as amount
Small data sets are always better
questions.explanation: The sentence says both the amount and quality of data matter.

questions.prompt: If three machines make three parts in three minutes, how many parts do six similar machines make in three minutes?
questions.explanation: Doubling the machines doubles the parts made in the same time, so they make 6 parts.

questions.prompt: Which choice shows the best lateral thinking?
choices: Asking whether the problem is framed correctly
questions.explanation: Lateral thinking often begins by questioning the framing of the problem.

stageGroups.title: Evidence and Assumptions
stageGroups.encouragement: Good reasoning. The next stage pushes your ability to hold several ideas at once.

questions.prompt: A survey says 80% of 10 people liked a product. What is the biggest caution?
choices: The sample may be too small
questions.explanation: A sample of 10 people is small, so the result may not generalise well.

questions.prompt: Which statement follows: If it rains, the match is cancelled. It is raining.
questions.explanation: The rule says rain causes cancellation, and it is raining.

questions.prompt: What is the missing number: 2, 6, 18, 54, ____?
questions.explanation: Each number is multiplied by 3, so 54 becomes 162.

questions.prompt: A result is described as 'consistent'. What does that most likely mean?
choices: It gives similar outcomes when repeated
questions.explanation: A consistent result is one that stays similar across repeated checks.

questions.prompt: A map scale says 1 unit represents 5 units of distance. How far is 4 map units?
questions.explanation: Four map units represent 4 times 5, which is 20 units.

questions.prompt: Which question best tests whether a claim is biased?
choices: Who benefits if people believe it?
questions.explanation: A useful bias check asks whether someone has an interest in the claim being accepted.

questions.prompt: If no musicians are pilots, and Lena is a musician, what must be true?
questions.explanation: If no musicians are pilots, then a musician such as Lena cannot be a pilot.

questions.prompt: Which choice is most likely to improve a weak argument?
choices: Add a clear reason with evidence
questions.explanation: A weak argument is usually improved by a clear reason supported by evidence.

stageGroups.title: Two-Step Reasoning
stageGroups.encouragement: That was a strong round. Now you are entering the harder end of the challenge.

questions.prompt: A price rises by 10% and then falls by 10%. Compared with the original price, what is true?
questions.explanation: A 10% fall after a 10% rise is taken from the larger amount, leaving the result slightly lower than the original.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion is best supported: Students who slept longer scored higher, but the study did not control for study time.
choices: Sleep may be linked to scores, but other factors could matter
questions.explanation: The link may be real, but without controlling for study time, causation is uncertain.

questions.prompt: Which number completes the pattern: 1, 4, 9, 16, ____?
questions.explanation: The numbers are square numbers: 1, 4, 9, 16, then 25.

questions.prompt: An argument says, 'Most people in this room agree, so the claim is true.' What is the weakness?
choices: Agreement alone does not prove truth
questions.explanation: A claim is not proved true simply because many people agree with it.

questions.prompt: Three friends each shake hands with every other friend once. How many handshakes occur?
questions.explanation: The handshakes are A-B, A-C, and B-C, making 3.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is the clearest definition?
choices: Energy is the capacity to do work or cause change
questions.explanation: It gives a specific meaning rather than a vague description or opinion.

questions.prompt: If all A are B, and some B are C, what must be true?
questions.explanation: The statement directly includes that some B are C; nothing definite follows about A and C.

questions.prompt: A problem seems impossible because one rule is assumed but never stated. What is the best first move?
questions.explanation: Finding hidden assumptions can reveal a new way to solve the problem.

stageGroups.title: Cambridge-Style Challenge
stageGroups.encouragement: Excellent. One final stage remains, and it rewards calm judgement over speed.

questions.prompt: A researcher finds that people who read more also have larger vocabularies. Which conclusion is safest?
choices: Reading may be associated with vocabulary size
Reading is the only cause of vocabulary size
People with larger vocabularies never read
questions.explanation: The finding shows an association, but it does not prove a single cause.

questions.prompt: What is the next number: 2, 3, 5, 9, 17, ____?
questions.explanation: The increases are 1, 2, 4, and 8, so the next increase is 16, giving 33.

questions.prompt: A statement is necessary for an argument if the argument fails without it. Which is the necessary assumption here: 'The bridge is safe because it passed inspection yesterday.'
choices: The inspection was capable of detecting major safety problems
The bridge is made of metal
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the inspection being able to detect serious safety issues.

questions.prompt: A group of 5 numbers has an average of 12. What is their total?
questions.explanation: Average equals total divided by count, so the total is 12 times 5, which is 60.

questions.prompt: Which criticism is most relevant to a study with no control group?
choices: It is harder to know what would have happened without the treatment
The title may be too short
questions.explanation: A control group helps compare the treatment against what happens without it.

questions.prompt: If every item in a set is either red or blue, and an item is not red, what must be true?
choices: It is both red and blue
questions.explanation: Within that set, anything not red must be blue.

questions.prompt: Which phrase best describes a counterexample?
choices: A case that challenges a general claim
A longer version of a claim
questions.explanation: A counterexample is a case that shows a general claim may not always hold.

questions.prompt: A student solves a problem by trying a simpler version first. Why can that help?
choices: It can reveal the underlying structure
It removes the need for logic
It guarantees the answer is small
questions.explanation: A simpler version can make the structure of the harder problem easier to see.

questions.prompt: A participant says, 'This solution is strong because it uses fewer assumptions.' Why might that matter?
choices: Fewer assumptions can make reasoning easier to test
Fewer assumptions make every answer correct
questions.explanation: Fewer assumptions can make an explanation clearer and easier to test.

questions.prompt: What number completes the pattern: 1, 2, 6, 24, 120, ____?
questions.explanation: The pattern multiplies by 2, then 3, then 4, then 5, so the next step is times 6, giving 720.

questions.prompt: Which statement is logically equivalent to: If a claim is proven, it has evidence?
choices: If a claim has no evidence, it is not proven
If a claim has evidence, it is proven
If a claim is not proven, it has no evidence
If a claim is popular, it is proven
questions.explanation: The statement with no evidence cannot be proven follows from the original rule.

questions.prompt: A fair coin is flipped twice. What is the probability of getting two heads?
questions.explanation: The four equally likely outcomes are HH, HT, TH, and TT, so HH is 1 out of 4.

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest objection to: 'No one complained, so everyone was satisfied'?
choices: People may be dissatisfied without complaining
questions.explanation: Silence does not necessarily mean satisfaction.

questions.prompt: A theory explains the evidence, but another theory explains the same evidence with fewer unsupported claims. Which is usually preferable?
choices: The theory with fewer unsupported claims
The theory with the longer name
The theory that sounds more dramatic
questions.explanation: When explanations fit the evidence equally well, fewer unsupported claims are usually preferred.

questions.prompt: Which answer best reflects intellectual humility?
choices: I may need to revise my view if better evidence appears
I am right because I said it first
Changing my mind is always weakness
Evidence matters only when it agrees with me
questions.explanation: Intellectual humility means being willing to update a view when better evidence appears.

questions.prompt: A puzzle has many details, but only two affect the answer. What is the best strategy?
questions.explanation: Hard reasoning often requires identifying which details actually matter.
